items.prompt_md: In the task-scheduler heap solution, why is a separate cooldown queue needed alongside the heap?
options: Because heapq cannot store negative numbers
Because a just-run task must wait n slots before it's eligible again, so it can't stay in the heap where it would be picked immediately
Because the heap can only hold a fixed number of elements
Because ties in count need to be broken alphabetically
items.answer: Because a just-run task must wait n slots before it's eligible again, so it can't stay in the heap where it would be picked immediately
items.explanation_md: The heap always pops the max-count entry, so a just-run task left inside it would simply be chosen again next round. Parking it in a FIFO cooldown queue until its wait is over keeps the heap holding only currently-eligible tasks.

items.prompt_md: Why does the scheduler heap store `-c` (negative counts) rather than the raw remaining count for each task?
options: Negative numbers save memory in Python
heapq is a min-heap, so negating the count makes the task with the largest remaining count sit at the root
It prevents ties between tasks with equal counts
It has no effect and is purely stylistic
items.answer: heapq is a min-heap, so negating the count makes the task with the largest remaining count sit at the root
items.explanation_md: The greedy rule needs the most-frequent remaining task each round; negating turns 'largest count' into 'smallest stored key', which is what a min-heap's root naturally gives you.

items.prompt_md: Why does the cooldown queue in the task scheduler need to be a FIFO (deque), not just any collection?
options: Because tasks always finish their cooldown in the same order they entered it (earliest ready_time first), so processing front-to-back is correct and O(1)
Because FIFO queues sort automatically by count
Because heapq requires a deque as a companion structure
Because a stack would run out of capacity for large n
items.answer: Because tasks always finish their cooldown in the same order they entered it (earliest ready_time first), so processing front-to-back is correct and O(1)
items.explanation_md: Since cooldown length n is fixed, ready_time increases in the same order tasks were appended, so the earliest-appended task is always the next one whose cooldown expires — exactly what a FIFO deque exposes at its front.

items.prompt_md: In the least_interval loop, why is `time` incremented even on slots where the heap is empty but the cooldown queue is not?
options: Because the schedule must still 'burn' idle slots waiting for a cooling-down task to become eligible again
It's a bug — time should only advance when a task actually runs
Because heapq requires a full slot before every push
Because idle slots don't count toward the final answer
items.answer: Because the schedule must still 'burn' idle slots waiting for a cooling-down task to become eligible again
items.explanation_md: If no task is currently eligible (heap empty) but something is still cooling down, the CPU must sit idle for that slot — it still counts toward total elapsed time.

items.prompt_md: What would go wrong if a just-run task were pushed straight back onto the heap instead of being routed through the cooldown queue?
options: Nothing — the heap would still behave correctly
It would likely be popped again on the very next round (since it's still the most frequent), violating the cooldown/spacing constraint
The heap would throw an error on duplicate counts
It would make the schedule shorter than necessary
items.answer: It would likely be popped again on the very next round (since it's still the most frequent), violating the cooldown/spacing constraint
items.explanation_md: The heap has no notion of eligibility — it just returns the max count. Skipping the cooldown queue would let the same task repeat before its spacing constraint is satisfied.

items.prompt_md: Why is re-scanning all task counts every round to find the current maximum considered wasteful compared to the heap approach?
options: It isn't wasteful — both approaches cost the same
A full scan costs O(k) per round for k distinct task types, recurring over the whole schedule, whereas a heap answers the same query in O(log k)
Scanning is only wasteful when there's no cooldown constraint
Scanning cannot find the maximum correctly
items.answer: A full scan costs O(k) per round for k distinct task types, recurring over the whole schedule, whereas a heap answers the same query in O(log k)
items.explanation_md: Repeating an O(k) scan every round throughout the whole schedule adds up; a heap keeps the 'current max' query down to O(log k) per round instead.

items.prompt_md: What is a common bug when implementing greedy scheduling with a heap and a cooldown queue?
options: Using a max-heap (via negation) on task counts
Forgetting to re-check the cooldown queue at the *current* time slot before advancing further, causing a task to re-enter the heap late (or a schedule that's too long)
Popping the most frequent task each round
Using a deque for the cooldown side queue
items.answer: Forgetting to re-check the cooldown queue at the *current* time slot before advancing further, causing a task to re-enter the heap late (or a schedule that's too long)
items.explanation_md: The cooldown queue must be checked every single time step so a task re-enters the heap the instant its wait ends — skipping or delaying that check produces an incorrect (too-long) schedule.

items.prompt_md: In merging k sorted lists with a heap, what does the heap's root represent at any point during the merge?
options: The largest remaining value across all k lists
The smallest not-yet-output value among the current fronts of all k lists
The average of the k list heads
An arbitrary element with no defined meaning
items.answer: The smallest not-yet-output value among the current fronts of all k lists
items.explanation_md: Each list contributes its current front to the heap; the root is always the globally smallest of those fronts — the correct next element to emit into the merged output.

items.prompt_md: Why is a heap a natural fit for 'repeatedly pick the best/next item' problems like merging k sorted lists or meeting rooms by earliest end time?
options: Because a heap always keeps its full contents in exact sorted order
Because a heap gives O(log k) access to the current extremum after every pick, letting you repeatedly re-derive 'what's best right now' without a full re-scan
Because a heap uses less memory than a hash map
Because heaps can only be used with numeric keys
items.answer: Because a heap gives O(log k) access to the current extremum after every pick, letting you repeatedly re-derive 'what's best right now' without a full re-scan
items.explanation_md: Every time you consume the current-best item, a heap cheaply produces the next-best in O(log k), which is exactly the repeated-selection shape these problems have — a heap is not internally fully sorted, but its root is always correct.

items.prompt_md: For meeting-rooms-style scheduling, why key a heap on each meeting's *end* time rather than its start time?
options: Start time is what determines when a room frees up, not end time
The heap needs to know which currently-occupied room frees up soonest, which is determined by the earliest end time among the meetings in progress
End time is always numerically smaller than start time
Keying by end time avoids needing a heap at all
items.answer: The heap needs to know which currently-occupied room frees up soonest, which is determined by the earliest end time among the meetings in progress
items.explanation_md: A room becomes available exactly when its current meeting ends, so tracking the minimum end time via a heap tells you the earliest moment a room can be reused.

items.prompt_md: With k distinct task types, what is the time complexity of each round's heap pop/push in the greedy task scheduler?
options: O(log k)
O(k)
O(k log k)
items.answer: O(log k)
items.explanation_md: The heap never holds more than k distinct task counts, so each push/pop is O(log k) regardless of how long the overall schedule runs.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of naively re-scanning all k task counts every single time slot to find the current max, across a schedule of total length T?
options: O(T)
O(T log k)
O(T k)
O(k)
items.answer: O(T k)
items.explanation_md: Scanning all k counts costs O(k), and doing that on every one of T time slots gives O(T k) total — much worse than O(T log k) with a heap.

items.prompt_md: What is the overall time complexity of merging k sorted linked lists using a heap of size k, where the lists have N total nodes?
options: O(N)
O(N log k)
O(N log N)
O(N k)
items.answer: O(N log k)
items.explanation_md: Each of the N total nodes is pushed and popped from a heap that never exceeds size k, so each node costs O(log k), giving O(N log k) overall.

items.prompt_md: Naively merging k sorted lists by concatenating them all and sorting from scratch costs what, in terms of N total elements?
options: O(N)
O(N log k)
O(N log N)
O(k log k)
items.answer: O(N log N)
items.explanation_md: A full sort of all N elements ignores the fact that each of the k lists is already sorted, costing O(N log N) instead of exploiting that structure with a heap for O(N log k).

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the heap used for greedy task scheduling, in terms of k distinct task types?
options: O(log k)
O(k)
O(n) for total tasks n
items.answer: O(k)
items.explanation_md: The heap holds one entry per distinct task type at most, so its size is bounded by k regardless of how many total task instances there are.

items.prompt_md: You must repeatedly assign the next incoming meeting to whichever room frees up soonest, given a list of meeting start/end times.
options: Maintain a min-heap of room end times, always assigning the room with the earliest end time
Sort meetings by start time and use two pointers between start and end lists
Build a hash map counting meetings per room
Use a stack to track meetings in arrival order
items.answer: Maintain a min-heap of room end times, always assigning the room with the earliest end time
items.explanation_md: Repeatedly needing 'which room becomes free soonest' is a running-minimum query best answered by a heap of end times.

items.prompt_md: A CPU must repeatedly run whichever process has the most remaining CPU-burst time, with a mandatory cooldown before any process can run again.
options: Sort processes once by burst time and cycle through the fixed order
Use a max-heap of remaining burst times, popping the largest each round and holding just-run processes in a cooldown queue
Use a hash map keyed by process id to track counts
Use two pointers over the process list
items.answer: Use a max-heap of remaining burst times, popping the largest each round and holding just-run processes in a cooldown queue
items.explanation_md: Greedy 'run the current best, but it's temporarily disqualified after running' is the heap-plus-cooldown-queue pattern from task scheduling.

items.prompt_md: You have k already-sorted arrays of numbers and need to merge them into one fully sorted array.
options: Maintain a size-k min-heap of the current front element from each array, always popping the smallest and advancing that array
Sort every array from scratch after concatenating them all
Use a hash map to count how many times each value appears across all arrays
Use a stack to reverse each array before combining
items.answer: Maintain a size-k min-heap of the current front element from each array, always popping the smallest and advancing that array
items.explanation_md: Repeatedly picking the smallest among k current candidates is the classic k-way-merge heap pattern, avoiding an O(N log N) full re-sort.

items.prompt_md: A print server must always print whichever queued job currently has the highest remaining page count, and a job that just printed can't be chosen again for the next 3 rounds.
options: Use a max-heap of remaining page counts, popping the largest each round and parking just-printed jobs in a cooldown queue
Sort jobs once by page count and print in that fixed order
Use a hash map keyed by job id to store page counts
Use two pointers scanning the job list from both ends
items.answer: Use a max-heap of remaining page counts, popping the largest each round and parking just-printed jobs in a cooldown queue
items.explanation_md: Greedily picking the current 'most needed' job under a temporary disqualification window is exactly the max-heap-plus-cooldown-queue setup.

items.prompt_md: Given a list of meeting intervals, determine the minimum number of rooms required to schedule all of them without overlap.
options: Maintain a min-heap of end times for rooms currently in use, reusing a room whenever the next meeting's start is >= the heap's minimum end time
Build a hash map of meeting id to room number
Use two pointers over the raw (unsorted) interval list
Use a stack to push and pop meetings by arrival order
items.answer: Maintain a min-heap of end times for rooms currently in use, reusing a room whenever the next meeting's start is >= the heap's minimum end time
items.explanation_md: Tracking 'which occupied room frees up next' as meetings are processed in start-time order is the min-heap-of-end-times pattern.

items.prompt_md: A job queue must repeatedly run whichever pending job has the earliest deadline, and no cooldown or spacing constraint applies at all.
options: Maintain a min-heap keyed by deadline, always popping the job with the earliest deadline
Build a hash map keyed by job id to track deadlines
Use two pointers scanning the job list from both ends
Use a stack to process jobs in LIFO order
items.answer: Maintain a min-heap keyed by deadline, always popping the job with the earliest deadline
items.explanation_md: Repeatedly extracting the current best (earliest deadline) job each round is a running-extremum query, which a heap answers in O(log k) with no cooldown queue needed here.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of task names, count how many times each distinct task appears — no scheduling order or cooldown is involved.
options: Maintain a max-heap of task counts
Build a hash map that counts occurrences of each task name in one pass
Use two pointers over the task array
Use a stack to track task order
items.answer: Build a hash map that counts occurrences of each task name in one pass
items.explanation_md: There's no repeated 'pick the current best under a constraint' step here, just a one-time exact frequency count — a hash map is the direct tool, not a heap.

items.prompt_md: Two already-sorted arrays of task priorities must be combined into one sorted array, with no picking-the-best-repeatedly-under-constraints logic involved beyond a simple merge.
options: Maintain a max-heap of task counts with a cooldown queue
Use two pointers, one per array, always taking the smaller of the two current elements
Build a hash map counting values across both arrays
Use a stack to reverse one array before merging
items.answer: Use two pointers, one per array, always taking the smaller of the two current elements
items.explanation_md: Merging exactly two already-sorted sequences is the two-pointers pattern; a heap is the right tool only once there are three or more sources competing for 'current smallest.'

items.prompt_md: Evaluate a fully-parenthesized arithmetic expression given as a string, respecting nested parentheses.
options: Maintain a min-heap of operator precedences
Use a stack to hold operators and operands, resolving the innermost parenthesized group first
Build a hash map from operator to precedence
Use two pointers scanning from each end of the string
items.answer: Use a stack to hold operators and operands, resolving the innermost parenthesized group first
items.explanation_md: Nested structure resolved innermost-first is the stack pattern's signature; there's no repeated best-of-k selection here, so a heap doesn't apply.

items.prompt_md: An event ticketing system must repeatedly release the currently-highest-demand ticket tier for sale, but a tier just released can't be released again for the next 2 rounds.
options: Sort tiers once by demand and release in that fixed order forever
Use a max-heap of remaining demand counts, popping the highest each round and holding just-released tiers in a cooldown queue
Build a hash map keyed by tier name to store demand counts
Use two pointers scanning the tier list from both ends
items.answer: Use a max-heap of remaining demand counts, popping the highest each round and holding just-released tiers in a cooldown queue
items.explanation_md: Greedily picking the current highest-demand tier each round under a temporary disqualification window is the same max-heap-plus-cooldown-queue pattern as task scheduling.
